meta.analysis: Type-Based Age Estimation — Great Circle On-line vs Off-line
meta.methodology: Each site assigned estimated founding midpoint based on its Megalithic Portal type classification and published archaeological date ranges. On-line = within 50km of Alison's Great Circle.
caveats: Type-based dating assigns the SAME midpoint to all sites of the same type, regardless of individual dating evidence
On-line sites are overwhelmingly non-European while the database is ~90% European, creating geographic confound
Midpoints flatten the actual date ranges, which can span thousands of years
The Megalithic Portal type system sometimes misclassifies sites (e.g., Egyptian mastabas as 'Chambered Cairn')

type_age_comparison.interpretation: By type-based estimation, on-line sites are 250 years YOUNGER than off-line sites by median. This reflects the fact that on-line sites include many 'Ancient Temple', 'Ancient Village', and 'Sculptured Stone' types which have broad date ranges with relatively recent midpoints, while the off-line European database is dominated by Neolithic-Bronze Age types with older midpoints.

all_on_line_sites_ranked_by_age.name: Possible Pyramids near Kafr Al Masallat

pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Mortar samples from Great Pyramid: 2871-2604 BCE (Bonani et al. 1995, Lehner/Wenke); older charcoal may be 'old wood effect'
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: Maadi culture settlement nearby (c. 3900-3500 BCE). Heit el-Ghurab workers' village shows occupation from ~2600 BCE. The Sphinx enclosure shows evidence of water erosion that geologist Robert Schoch argues dates to periods of heavy rainfall (pre-3000 BCE), though this is disputed by most Egyptologists.
pre_construction_evidence.controversial_older_dates: Robert Schoch (1991/2012): water weathering on Sphinx enclosure suggests 5000-7000 BCE or older. Robert Bauval/Adrian Gilbert (1994): shaft alignments to ~10,500 BCE precession position. John Anthony West: argued for pre-dynastic construction. These are rejected by mainstream Egyptology.
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: The Sphinx sits on bedrock; no earlier structure beneath it. However, the Giza plateau shows evidence of an earlier mastaba cemetery (Tomb of Heminu) and the site was clearly chosen for its geological properties. The Great Pyramid's hill contains a natural rocky knoll incorporated into the structure.

pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Radiocarbon dates from mortars: range 2800-2500 BCE (consistent with 3rd Dynasty). Wood samples from Djoser complex: 2650±100 BCE
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: 2nd Dynasty royal tombs (Hotepsekhemwy, Raneb, Nynetjer) found at Saqqara, dating to c. 2890-2686 BCE. 1st Dynasty tombs also present. The site has continuous use from at least Early Dynastic Period (c. 3100 BCE). Archaic mastaba cemetery predates Djoser.
pre_construction_evidence.controversial_older_dates: None significantly. The site's early dynastic use is well-documented and uncontroversial.
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: YES. Djoser's Step Pyramid was built over/near earlier mastaba structures. The subterranean galleries beneath contain 40,000+ stone vessels, many from 1st-2nd Dynasty, clearly pre-dating the pyramid. Some archaeologists believe the step pyramid began as a mastaba that was expanded.

pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Limited direct dating. Pyramid construction assigned by king-lists and stratigraphy to 5th Dynasty.
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: The site was used for elite burials from at least the 4th Dynasty. Lepsius Pyramid XXIV may date to late 4th Dynasty. Sun temples at Abu Ghurob nearby are early 5th Dynasty. No evidence of pre-Old Kingdom use at this specific site.
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: Some 5th Dynasty pyramids were built near or over earlier tombs, but no dramatically older construction beneath the visible monuments.

pre_construction_evidence.visible_construction_date: Various periods, primarily Pre-Pottery Neolithic B to Chalcolithic (7500-3500 BCE), with some Early Bronze Age additions
pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Avner (1984, 2001): standing stones in the Uvda Valley dated to PPNB, ~7000-6000 BCE by associated material. Some Negev desert cult sites yield dates as early as 8th millennium BCE from associated hearths/floors.
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: The Negev has continuous human presence from the Natufian period (c. 12,000 BCE). Many standing stone sites are associated with campsites dating to the PPNB (8th-7th millennium BCE). The matsevot tradition appears to begin in the PPNB and continue through the Chalcolithic.
pre_construction_evidence.controversial_older_dates: Some researchers argue for Epi-Paleolithic origins of the standing stone tradition (pre-10,000 BCE) based on association with Natufian sites, but direct dating of the stones themselves is impossible — only associated materials can be dated.
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: Many matsevot sites show multiple phases of erection and re-erection, with older stones sometimes incorporated into later arrangements. The tradition is remarkably continuous over thousands of years.

pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Wooden stakes marking line endpoints: 525 BCE ± 80 (Clarkson & Dorn 1999). Most lines date to 200 BCE - 600 CE. OSL dating of cleared surfaces: some lines 400 BCE.
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: The Nasca drainage was occupied from at least the Initial Period (~1800 BCE). Paracas culture (800-100 BCE) created earlier geoglyphs in the same region (Palpa lines). Cahuachi ceremonial center nearby dates from ~400 BCE. Pernil Alto settlement: ~3800 BCE.
pre_construction_evidence.controversial_older_dates: Some Palpa geoglyphs may date to Paracas period (800-200 BCE), making them older than the Nasca lines proper. Recent German-Peruvian research has found geoglyphs pre-dating the Nasca culture by centuries.
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: The lines are surface features (removal of dark rocks to expose light ground). Earlier Paracas-era geoglyphs exist in the broader Nazca-Palpa region, suggesting a continuous tradition. The Nazca pampa shows no built structures underneath the lines.

pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Earliest reliable settlement dates: c. 1200 CE (Hunt & Lipo 2006, revised from earlier estimates). Anakena beach settlement: 1200-1250 CE. Ahu construction peaks 1400-1600 CE.
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: Revised chronology (Hunt & Lipo 2006, 2011): initial colonization ~1200 CE, not the earlier ~400-800 CE as previously claimed. There is no reliable evidence of pre-Polynesian occupation. Earlier dates (Heyerdahl: pre-400 CE) are now attributed to old carbon in soils.
pre_construction_evidence.controversial_older_dates: Thor Heyerdahl proposed South American contact and earlier dates. Some contested charcoal dates suggest ~400 CE occupation. The oral tradition of 'Long Ears' and 'Short Ears' has been interpreted as evidence of multiple colonization waves, but this is not supported by genetic or archaeological evidence.
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: Some ahu show rebuilding phases. Ahu Vinapu's fitted stonework has been compared to Inca masonry, leading to speculation about South American contact, but mainstream archaeology attributes this to independent development. No pre-Polynesian structures have been identified.

pre_construction_evidence.visible_construction_date: c. 518-330 BCE (Achaemenid Empire, Darius I through Artaxerxes III)
pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Construction well-dated by royal inscriptions. Darius I's foundation tablets: 518 BCE. Limited C14 dating needed given rich textual record.
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: Tall-e Bakun nearby: continuous occupation from c. 5000-3000 BCE (one of the longest-occupied sites in Iran). The Marvdasht Plain surrounding Persepolis has evidence of occupation from at least the 6th millennium BCE. Anshan (Tall-e Malyan), the Elamite capital, is nearby.
pre_construction_evidence.controversial_older_dates: None for Persepolis itself — the construction date is well-established. However, the broader Marvdasht Plain has extremely deep prehistoric roots (Bakun, Mushki, Jari cultures from 7th-5th millennia BCE).
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: Some evidence of pre-Achaemenid settlement on the Persepolis terrace, but the massive platform construction of Darius largely obliterated earlier remains. Excavations have found some pre-Achaemenid pottery beneath terrace fill.

pre_construction_evidence.visible_construction_date: Hero stones: primarily 7th-17th century CE. The Jaisalmer fort: 1156 CE.
pre_construction_evidence.earliest_radiocarbon: Limited radiocarbon data for the hero stones specifically. The Thar Desert region shows human occupation from Lower Paleolithic. Megalithic traditions in Rajasthan broadly date to Iron Age (c. 1000 BCE - 500 CE).
pre_construction_evidence.pre_construction_occupation: The Thar Desert has evidence of human settlement from at least 100,000 years ago (Acheulean tools). Kalibangan (Indus Valley Civilization), ~200km away, dates to c. 3500-1750 BCE. The broader region has Ahar-Banas culture sites from 3rd millennium BCE.
pre_construction_evidence.controversial_older_dates: Some researchers propose much older dates for megaliths in the Thar region, but these are not well-supported by systematic dating campaigns.
pre_construction_evidence.earlier_structures_underneath: The hero stone tradition is a surface/standing monument tradition. No evidence of dramatically older structures beneath the visible stones.
